reasoning: The informal statement asks about "ordered field" — meaning there is no linear order on ℂ compatible with its field operations. The CORRECTED formalization captures this correctly via `IsStrictOrderedRing ℂ` (which requires the order to be compatible with ring operations). The ORIGINAL formalization uses `IsLinearOrder ℂ r`, which only requires that `r` be a linear order (reflexive, transitive, antisymmetric, total) with NO compatibility requirement with the ring/field operations. This is a strictly stronger claim (no linear order at all trivially implies no compatible linear order). However, ℂ has the same cardinality as ℝ, so there clearly exists SOME linear order on ℂ (e.g., pulled back through a bijection with ℝ, or lexicographic order). Therefore `∃ r, IsLinearOrder ℂ r` is true, and the ORIGINAL statement `¬ ∃ r, IsLinearOrder ℂ r` is provably FALSE. The error is a misunderstanding of the informal statement — the author missed that "ordered field" requires order-ring compatibility, not merely a linear order on the underlying set.
